title: Invariant measures for nonlinear SPDE's: uniqueness and stability
overall_summary: Maslowski and Seidler survey criteria for uniqueness, ergodicity, and asymptotic stability of invariant measures for stochastic evolution equations in infinite-dimensional spaces, especially SPDEs. The paper distinguishes strong asymptotic stability, meaning convergence of transition laws in total variation, from weak asymptotic stability, meaning narrow convergence of measures; either form of global stability implies uniqueness of an invariant probability measure. The most directly useful route for the original problem is the abstract Doob-type criterion: on a Polish state space, if positive-time transition probabilities are mutually equivalent, then any invariant probability measure is unique and the transition laws converge to it in total variation; strong Feller plus irreducibility is a standard way to obtain this equivalence. The paper also records several mechanisms for proving strong Feller/irreducibility for semilinear SPDEs with additive noise, including Girsanov comparison with the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, support/approximate controllability arguments, Kolmogorov-equation smoothing, and Bismut-Elworthy derivative formulae. As alternatives when total-variation methods are unavailable, it reviews Lyapunov and dissipativity/remote-start methods yielding weak convergence and uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1 (linear Gaussian strong asymptotic stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a real separable Hilbert space, let A: Dom(A) ⊆ H → H generate a strongly continuous semigroup (e^{At})_{t≥0}, and let W_t be a Wiener process on H with incremental covariance operator Q ∈ L(H), possibly cylindrical. Assume that for some T>0, ∫_0^T ||e^{At} Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2 dt < ∞, so the linear stochastic equation dZ_t = A Z_t dt + dW_t has the mild solution Z_t = e^{At}x + ∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)} dW_r and transition law P_t(x,·)=N(e^{At}x,Q_t), where Q_t=∫_0^t e^{Ar}Qe^{A*r}dr. In the proposition the paper assumes, for simplicity, Q>0. Let K be a linear subspace of H such that for every x∈K there is t_0(x)≥0 with e^{At}x ∈ Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for all t>t_0(x), and ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}x|| → 0 as t→∞. Then for all x,y∈K, the total variation distance |||P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·)||| tends to 0 as t→∞. If, in addition, sup_{t≥0} Tr Q_t < ∞, so that the linear equation has invariant Gaussian measure μ_* = N(0,Q_∞) with Q_∞=lim_{t→∞}Q_t, and if μ_*(K)=1, then |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| → 0 for every x∈K. Consequently μ_* is the only invariant probability measure concentrated on K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this proposition is proved by direct computation using the Cameron-Martin formula for Gaussian measures. Since P_t(x,·) and P_t(y,·) are Gaussian measures with the same covariance Q_t and means e^{At}x and e^{At}y, their mutual absolute continuity is governed by whether e^{At}(x-y) lies in the Cameron-Martin space Im(Q_t^{1/2}). Under the range hypothesis, the Radon-Nikodym derivative is the usual Cameron-Martin exponential with shift norm ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}(x-y)||. The second hypothesis forces this shift norm to vanish as t→∞, which implies convergence of the translated Gaussian measures in total variation. For convergence to μ_*, use invariance μ_*P_t=μ_* and μ_*(K)=1: formally, |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| = |||P_t(x,·)-∫_K P_t(y,·) μ_*(dy)||| ≤ ∫_K |||P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·)||| μ_*(dy), and dominated convergence gives the limit 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2 (linear range criterion for uniqueness and total-variation convergence)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same linear Hilbert-space setting as Proposition 1, assume ∫_0^T ||e^{At}Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2 dt < ∞ for some T>0 and sup_{t≥0} Tr Q_t < ∞, so that μ_*=N(0,Q_∞) is an invariant Gaussian measure. Suppose that for every t>0, Im(e^{At}) ⊆ Im(Q_t^{1/2}). Then |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| → 0 as t→∞ for every x∈H. In particular, μ_* is the only invariant probability measure for the linear equation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents this as a corollary of the abstract equivalence theorem later stated as Theorem 4. The inclusion Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) is exactly the Cameron-Martin/Hájek-Feldman criterion ensuring that the Gaussian transition measures P_t(x,·)=N(e^{At}x,Q_t) are mutually equivalent for positive times. Once positive-time transition probabilities are equivalent and an invariant probability μ_* exists, Theorem 4 yields total-variation convergence of P_t(x,·) to μ_* and uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3 (spectral check for parabolic linear equations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be self-adjoint, negative, and have compact resolvent on H. Let {e_j}_{j≥1} be an orthonormal basis such that Ae_j=-α_j e_j, with 0<α_j→∞. Suppose Qe_j=λ_j e_j, where 0<λ_j≤λ_0<∞. Then the mild solution and invariant Gaussian measure for dZ_t=AZ_tdt+dW_t exist if ∑_{j=1}^∞ λ_j/α_j < ∞. The range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) from Proposition 2 is equivalent, in this diagonal setting, to boundedness for every t>0 of the sequence { (α_i/λ_i) exp(-2α_i t) }_{i∈N}. In particular, for the heat equation ∂_t u = ∂_ξ^2 u + η on (0,1) with Dirichlet boundary conditions, H=L^2(0,1), A=∂_ξ^2, and space-time white noise corresponding to Q=I, the criterion is satisfied.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the diagonal basis, Q_t is diagonal with eigenvalues q_{j,t}=λ_j(1-exp(-2α_jt))/(2α_j). Thus Tr Q_∞ = ∑_j λ_j/(2α_j), giving the summability criterion for existence of the invariant Gaussian law. The inclusion Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) reduces to a coordinatewise boundedness condition: applying e^{At} multiplies the j-th coordinate by exp(-α_jt), while Q_t^{1/2} multiplies by q_{j,t}^{1/2}. Hence the relevant ratio is exp(-2α_jt)/q_{j,t}, equivalent for fixed t>0 to (α_j/λ_j)exp(-2α_jt), up to harmless constants and the factor 1-exp(-2α_jt). For Q=I and the one-dimensional Dirichlet Laplacian, α_j grows quadratically, so ∑1/α_j<∞ and α_j exp(-2α_jt) is bounded for each t>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4 (abstract equivalence criterion: ergodic theorem and strong asymptotic stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ((X_t)_{t≥0},(P^x)_{x∈H}) be a Markov process in a Polish space H with transition probability function P_t(x,·), t≥0, x∈H. Assume it has an invariant probability measure μ_*. Assume further that all positive-time transition probabilities P_t(x,·), with t>0 and x∈H, are mutually equivalent. Then: (i) for each bounded Borel function φ:H→R and every x∈H, lim_{T→∞} (1/T)∫_0^T φ(X_t)dt = ∫_H φ dμ_* holds P^x-a.s.; (ii) for every x∈H, |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| → 0 as t→∞. In particular, μ_* is the unique invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The review cites this result as obtained independently by Stettner and Seidler and uses it as an abstract black box. The mechanism is the classical Doob/Khasminskii principle: mutual equivalence of positive-time kernels implies that null sets for one transition law are null for all, and, after averaging against the invariant measure, the transition probabilities are equivalent to μ_* at positive times. This eliminates nontrivial invariant classes and gives ergodicity of μ_*. The strong form of Doob's theorem for Markov processes on Polish spaces then yields total-variation convergence of P_t(x,·) to μ_* for every initial state, and the pointwise ergodic theorem follows for bounded Borel observables. Uniqueness is immediate: if ν were another invariant probability measure, then νP_t=ν, while P_t(x,·)→μ_* in total variation for every x, forcing ν=∫P_t(x,·)ν(dx)→μ_*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5 (Girsanov criterion for semilinear additive equations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear Hilbert-space stochastic evolution equation dX_t = AX_t dt + f(X_t)dt + dW_t, where A and W are as in the linear equation and f:H→Im(Q^{1/2}) satisfies the Lipschitz-type condition ||Q^{-1/2}(f(x)-f(y))|| ≤ K||x-y|| for all x,y∈H and some K<∞. Let P_t be the transition function of this nonlinear equation and let ~P_t be the transition function of the linear equation dZ_t=AZ_tdt+dW_t. Assume ∫_0^T ||e^{At}Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2dt<∞ for some T>0 and assume the linear range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for every t>0. Then for every t>0 and x∈H, P_t(x,·) and ~P_t(x,·) are equivalent measures. If, moreover, the nonlinear equation has an invariant probability measure μ_*, then μ_* is strongly asymptotically stable: |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*|||→0 for every x∈H; in particular it is unique.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The range condition gives mutual equivalence of the linear Gaussian transition measures by the Cameron-Martin/Hájek-Feldman theorem. The condition f(H)⊆Im(Q^{1/2}) with the displayed Lipschitz bound is exactly what is needed to apply the infinite-dimensional Girsanov theorem. The Girsanov density over a time interval [0,T] has the form exp{ ∫_0^T ⟨Q^{-1/2}f(Z_t),dW_t⟩ - (1/2)∫_0^T ||Q^{-1/2}f(Z_t)||^2dt }, where Z is the linear solution. Thus the nonlinear path law is absolutely continuous with respect to the linear path law on finite time intervals, and the same holds for time-t marginals; applying the argument in the reverse direction gives equivalence. Therefore all nonlinear transition probabilities are mutually equivalent, and Theorem 4 gives strong asymptotic stability and uniqueness whenever an invariant probability exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6 (strong Feller plus irreducibility implies equivalence of transition probabilities)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a Polish space and let P_t(x,·) be the transition function of a Markov process on H. The process is called strong Feller in the paper if, for each t>0 and each Borel set Γ⊆H, the function x↦P_t(x,Γ) is continuous. It is called irreducible if P_t(x,U)>0 for each t>0, each x∈H, and each nonempty open set U⊆H. If the process is strong Feller and irreducible, then the measures P_t(x,·), for all t>0 and x∈H, are mutually equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the standard support-continuity argument. Suppose P_t(x,A)=0 for a Borel set A. To compare with P_s(y,A), choose r with 0<r<min{s,t}. By Chapman-Kolmogorov, 0=P_t(x,A)=∫_H P_r(z,A)P_{t-r}(x,dz). The function g(z)=P_r(z,A) is nonnegative and continuous by the strong Feller property. If g(z_0)>0 for some z_0, then by continuity g is positive on a nonempty open neighborhood U of z_0; irreducibility gives P_{t-r}(x,U)>0, contradicting the integral being zero. Hence P_r(z,A)=0 for all z. Applying Chapman-Kolmogorov again, P_s(y,A)=∫_H P_r(z,A)P_{s-r}(y,dz)=0. Thus any null set for one positive-time transition probability is null for all, proving mutual equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7 (support/approximate-controllability criterion for irreducibility)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear additive equation dX_t=AX_tdt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t in a Hilbert space H, where A and W are as in the linear equation and f:H→H is Lipschitz continuous. For a fixed T>0 define the linear control operator K:L^2(0,T;H)→C_0([0,T];H) by (Kψ)(t)=∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}Q^{1/2}ψ(r)dr, where C_0([0,T];H)={g∈C([0,T];H):g(0)=0}. If Im(K) is dense in C_0([0,T];H), then the transition probability at time T is irreducible in the sense that P_T(x,U)>0 for every x∈H and every nonempty open U⊆H. If this density condition holds for every T>0, then the transition probability function is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the solution as X_t=u(t,x;~Z), where ~Z_t=∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}dW_r is the linear stochastic convolution with zero initial condition, and u(·,x;φ) solves the deterministic integral equation u(t,x;φ)=e^{At}x+∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}f(u(r,x;φ))dr+φ(t). Since f is Lipschitz, φ↦u(·,x;φ) is continuous from C_0([0,T];H) to C([0,T];H). The law of the Gaussian process ~Z on C_0([0,T];H) has closed support equal to the closure of its reproducing kernel Hilbert space, which is the closure of Im(K); by assumption, the support is all of C_0([0,T];H). Given any target open set U at time T, choose a continuous path η with η(0)=x and η(T)∈U, and set φ(t)=η(t)-e^{At}x-∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}f(η(r))dr; then u(·,x;φ)=η. Continuity gives a neighborhood of φ whose images at time T lie in U, and the full support of ~Z gives positive probability for that neighborhood. Hence P_T(x,U)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8 (strong Feller property via the infinite-dimensional Kolmogorov equation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let the linear equation dZ_t=AZ_tdt+dW_t satisfy ∫_0^T ||e^{At}Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2dt<∞ for some T>0 and sup_{t≥0}Tr Q_t<∞, and let μ=N(0,Q_∞) be its invariant Gaussian measure. Let T_t be the corresponding Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Markov semigroup on L^2(H,μ), and let P_t be the Markov semigroup of the semilinear additive equation dX_t=AX_tdt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t. Consider the mild backward Kolmogorov equation u(t,·)=T_tφ+∫_0^t T_{t-s}⟨f,Du(s,·)⟩ds. Assume f:H→H is bounded and continuous, assume the linear range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for t>0, and assume the stronger smoothing condition ∫_0^T ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}||_{L(H)}dt<∞ for some T>0. Then for every bounded Borel φ on H there exists a unique solution u to the mild Kolmogorov equation and u(t,·)∈C_b^1(H) for t>0. Moreover, u(t,x)=P_tφ(x)=E^xφ(X_t) for t>0 and x∈H whenever φ∈C_b(H). In particular, |||P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·)||| ≤ γ(t)||x-y|| for t>0 and x,y∈H, where γ(t)=sup{||DP_tφ(z)||: z∈H, φ∈C_b(H), |φ|≤1}<∞; hence the transition semigroup is strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Da Prato-Zabczyk and Chojnowska-Michalik-Goldys for the proof. The key input is that the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup T_t is smoothing under the range and integrability condition involving Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}; this gives bounds on derivatives of T_tφ even when φ is merely bounded. One then solves the mild Kolmogorov equation by a fixed-point/variation-of-constants argument, using boundedness of f to control the perturbation term T_{t-s}⟨f,Du(s)⟩. Identification of the mild Kolmogorov solution with the Markov semigroup P_t follows first for sufficiently regular or continuous φ and then by approximation. The derivative bound implies |P_tφ(x)-P_tφ(y)|≤γ(t)||x-y|| for bounded test functions φ; taking the supremum over |φ|≤1 gives the displayed total-variation Lipschitz estimate and therefore the strong Feller property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9 (Elworthy/Bismut-type derivative formula)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear additive Hilbert-space equation dX_t=AX_tdt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t. Assume Q is boundedly invertible, f is Lipschitz continuous and Gateaux differentiable on H, and the Gateaux derivative is continuous as a map from H into L(H) endowed with the strong operator topology. Then for every t>0 and every bounded Borel function φ, P_tφ∈C^1(H), and for x,h∈H, ⟨DP_tφ(x),h⟩ = (1/t) E^x[ φ(X_t) ∫_0^t ⟨Q^{-1/2}X_s^h,dW_s⟩ ], where X_s^h is the L^2-directional derivative of the solution X_s with respect to the initial condition in direction h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Da Prato-Elworthy-Zabczyk for this formula. The derivative process X_s^h is obtained by differentiating the stochastic evolution equation with respect to the initial condition, yielding the linear variational equation driven by the derivative of f along the solution. A stochastic integration-by-parts or Bismut-Elworthy argument expresses the spatial derivative of the transition semigroup without differentiating φ: the derivative is transferred to a stochastic integral involving Q^{-1/2}X_s^h. Since Q^{-1/2} is bounded and the variational equation can be estimated in L^2, the right-hand side gives bounds on ||DP_tφ(x)|| in terms of ||φ||_∞. Such bounds imply strong Feller regularization by showing that P_t maps bounded Borel functions to continuous, indeed differentiable, functions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10 (Lyapunov criterion for weak asymptotic stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space and consider dX_t={AX_t+f(X_t)}dt+σ(X_t)dW_t, where A:Dom(A)→H generates a C_0-semigroup, W is a Wiener process in another real separable Hilbert space U with nuclear covariance operator Q, and f:H→H and σ:H→L(U,H) are globally Lipschitz continuous. Let P_t be the associated transition semigroup. Suppose there exists V∈C^2(H;R) such that: (i) V(0)=0 and inf_{||y||≥r}V(y)>0 for every r>0; (ii) there are k<∞ and p>0 such that V(y)+||DV(y)||+||D^2V(y)|| ≤ k(1+||y||^p) for all y∈H; (iii) there exists a>0 such that for all x,y∈Dom(A), ⟨DV(x-y),Ax-Ay+f(x)-f(y)⟩ + (1/2)Tr{(σ(x)-σ(y))^*D^2V(x-y)(σ(x)-σ(y))Q} ≤ -aV(x-y). Then P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·) converges narrowly to 0 as t→∞ for every x,y∈H. If an invariant probability measure exists, then it is globally asymptotically stable for narrow convergence and therefore unique.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a Lyapunov argument applied to the difference of two solutions driven by the same noise. For sufficiently regular approximations, Itô's formula applied to V(X_t^x-X_t^y) gives a drift term exactly of the form in condition (iii), hence d/dt E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)]≤-a E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)]. Gronwall's lemma yields exponential decay of E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)]. Condition (i) turns small V into small Hilbert norm, so the coupled solutions converge in probability as t→∞. The polynomial bounds in (ii) justify the Itô formula/approximation and integrability steps for mild solutions. For any bounded continuous φ, uniform continuity on large balls plus tightness estimates and convergence in probability give Eφ(X_t^x)-Eφ(X_t^y)→0, i.e. narrow convergence of P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·) to zero. If μ is invariant, integrate the convergence against μ(dy) to obtain P_t(x,·)⇒μ, and uniqueness follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Quoted sufficient condition after Theorem 10 (norm-power Lyapunov function)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper quotes Corollary 2.8 of Maslowski [49]: in the setting of Theorem 10, the hypotheses of Theorem 10 are fulfilled with the natural choice V=||·||^p for a suitable p>0, provided the following hold. There exist β∈R and α≥0 such that ⟨Ax,x⟩≤β||x||^2 for all x∈Dom(A), ||Q^{1/2}(σ(x)-σ(y))^*(x-y)|| ≥ α||x-y||^2 for all x,y∈H, and β+Lip(f)+(1/2)Lip(σ)^2 Tr Q < α^2. Here Lip(·) denotes the Lipschitz constant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reproduce the proof, but the verification consists of substituting V(z)=||z||^p into the drift inequality of Theorem 10. The first displayed inequality controls the contribution of A to the derivative of ||X_t^x-X_t^y||^p. The Lipschitz constants of f and σ control the drift and diffusion growth terms. The lower bound involving Q^{1/2}(σ(x)-σ(y))^*(x-y) supplies a coercive noise contribution in the second-derivative term. The strict inequality β+Lip(f)+(1/2)Lip(σ)^2Tr Q<α^2 gives the negative coefficient required in Theorem 10(iii).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11 (dissipativity/remote-start theorem for additive noise)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space and consider the additive-noise equation dX_t=(AX_t+f(X_t))dt+σ dW_t, where W is a standard cylindrical Wiener process in a Hilbert space U, σ∈L(U,H), and A:Dom(A)→H is a closed linear operator. For a Banach space E, write ∂||x||_E for the subdifferential of the norm. A map γ:Dom(γ)⊆E→E is dissipative if for every x,y∈Dom(γ) there is z^*∈∂||x-y||_E with z^*(γ(x)-γ(y))≤0; it is m-dissipative if Im(λI-γ)=E for some λ>0. If G⊆E is a subspace, γ_G denotes the part of γ on G. The theorem assumes the standard well-posedness/Feller hypotheses listed in the paper: (1) there exists η∈R such that A-ηI and f-ηI are m-dissipative on H; (2) there exists a reflexive Banach space K densely and continuously embedded in H such that (A-ηI)_K and (f-ηI)_K are m-dissipative on K; (3) Dom(f)⊇K and f maps bounded sets in K into bounded sets in H; (4) the stochastic convolution W_A(t)=∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}σdW(r) is Dom(f_K)-valued, has paths continuous in H, and for every T>0, sup_{t∈[0,T]}{||W_A(t)||_K+||f(W_A(t))||_K}<∞ almost surely. In addition, suppose there are ω_1,ω_2∈R with ω=ω_1+ω_2>0 such that A+ω_1I and f+ω_2I are dissipative on H, and suppose sup_{t≥0} E[||W_A(t)||_H+||f(W_A(t))||_H] < ∞. Then the equation has a unique invariant probability measure μ, and P_t(y,·) converges narrowly to μ for every y∈H. Moreover, there exists L<∞ such that for every y∈H, t>0, and bounded Lipschitz g:H→R, |∫_H g dP_t(y,·)-∫_H g dμ| ≤ L(1+||y||)e^{-ωt/2}Lip(g).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper sketches the proof, known as the remote start method. Extend the driving cylindrical Wiener process to the whole real line by setting ~W(t)=W(t) for t≥0 and ~W(t)=Y(-t) for t<0, where Y is an independent standard cylindrical Wiener process. Let X(t;s,y), t≥s, denote the solution started from y at time s. First prove the a priori estimate E||X(t;s,y)||≤c+||y|| for s<0 and t≥s. To do this, subtract the stochastic convolution from time s: Ψ(t)=X(t;s,y)-∫_s^t e^{A(t-r)}σd~W(r). Then Ψ solves pathwise dΨ/dt=AΨ+f(Ψ+∫_s^t e^{A(t-r)}σd~W(r)), Ψ(s)=y. The dissipativity of A+ω_1I and f+ω_2I yields the Dini-derivative inequality d^-/dt ||Ψ(t)|| ≤ -ω||Ψ(t)|| + ||f(∫_s^t e^{A(t-r)}σd~W(r))||. The uniform expectation assumption on the convolution and f of the convolution gives the estimate. Next, for v<s<0 one obtains E||X(t;s,y)-X(t;v,y)|| ≤ e^{-ω(t-s)}(2||y||+c), t≥s. Hence {X(0;s,y):s≤0} is Cauchy in L^1(Ω;H) as s→-∞; let p be its limit and set μ=Law(p). By stationarity of the two-sided construction, P_t^*δ_y=Law(X(t;0,y))=Law(X(0;-t,y)) converges narrowly to Law(p)=μ. Since the Markov process is Feller, P_s^*μ=P_s^*(lim_{t→∞}P_t^*δ_y)=lim_{t→∞}P_{t+s}^*δ_y=μ, so μ is invariant. The exponential estimate for bounded Lipschitz g follows from the same Cauchy estimate by comparing X(t;0,y) with the remote-start limit. Uniqueness follows because every initial law is attracted narrowly to μ.

proof_techniques.name: Doob-type uniqueness from equivalence of transition kernels
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses an abstract Markov-process theorem on Polish spaces: if an invariant probability measure exists and all positive-time transition probabilities are mutually absolutely continuous, then the invariant measure is unique, pointwise ergodic averages converge almost surely, and transition laws converge in total variation to the invariant measure. This avoids constructing the invariant measure explicitly and reduces uniqueness to a positivity/equivalence property of the semigroup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The state space C_0([0,1]) is Polish, so this criterion can be applied directly if one proves mutual equivalence of P_t(u_0,·) for t>0. A practical route suggested by the paper is to prove strong Feller and irreducibility for the SPDE semigroup and then invoke Lemma 6 plus Theorem 4.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller plus irreducibility implies kernel equivalence
proof_techniques.description: The paper records the standard argument that strong Feller regularity turns hitting probabilities of Borel sets into continuous functions, while irreducibility prevents a nonnegative continuous hitting probability from being positive somewhere but integrating to zero from another point. This proves that all positive-time transition probabilities share the same null sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirichlet stochastic heat equation with additive space-time white noise, irreducibility is often accessible via support of the stochastic convolution, and strong Feller may be attacked through smoothing of the nondegenerate noise or through approximation by the smooth drifts G_{1/n}.

proof_techniques.name: Gaussian support and approximate controllability for irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: For semilinear additive equations, write the solution as a deterministic continuous map of the stochastic convolution. The law of the stochastic convolution has support equal to the closure of its reproducing kernel Hilbert space; if the corresponding deterministic control operator has dense range in the path space, then every deterministic controlled path can be approximated with positive probability, yielding irreducibility.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The additive Dirichlet heat noise has a rich stochastic convolution. A solver can try to formulate a support theorem in C([0,T],C_0([0,1])) for the heat convolution and combine it with continuity of the solution map, perhaps first for the G_{1/n}-regularized equations and then through the assumed approximation result.

proof_techniques.name: Girsanov comparison with the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process
proof_techniques.description: When the nonlinear drift f takes values in Im(Q^{1/2}) and Q^{-1/2}f satisfies suitable Lipschitz/growth bounds, the semilinear additive equation is absolutely continuous with respect to the linear Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation on finite time intervals. If the linear transition laws are mutually equivalent, the nonlinear transition laws are too, and uniqueness follows from the abstract equivalence criterion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For each smooth approximation G_{1/n}, the drift is a bounded Lipschitz Nemytskii-type term, and with space-time white noise one has the formally nondegenerate covariance Q=I in an L^2 setting. This suggests proving uniqueness for approximating semigroups via Girsanov and investigating whether equivalence or enough positivity survives the singular limit.

proof_techniques.name: Kolmogorov-equation smoothing criterion for strong Feller
proof_techniques.description: The paper describes a method based on mild solutions of the infinite-dimensional backward Kolmogorov equation. Under a nondegeneracy/smoothing condition such as ∫_0^T ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}||dt<∞ and bounded continuous drift, the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup regularizes enough to solve the Kolmogorov equation with C_b^1 spatial regularity. The derivative bound yields a Lipschitz estimate in total variation for transition kernels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The one-dimensional Dirichlet heat semigroup with space-time white noise satisfies the spectral nondegeneracy pattern in Example 3. This technique is a candidate for proving strong Feller for regularized equations and possibly for the limiting distributional-drift equation if the ABLM approximation theory gives sufficient convergence of semigroups.

proof_techniques.name: Bismut-Elworthy derivative formula
proof_techniques.description: Instead of solving a Kolmogorov PDE, the paper records a derivative formula for P_tφ in terms of a stochastic integral involving the variational derivative of the solution with respect to its initial condition. The formula differentiates the semigroup without differentiating φ and can establish strong Feller estimates when Q^{-1/2} is bounded and the variational equation is controlled.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For smooth G_{1/n} drifts one may derive variational equations and Bismut-type gradient estimates. Uniform estimates in n, if available, would be a possible route to strong Feller regularity of the limiting semigroup.

proof_techniques.name: Lyapunov stability of synchronized solutions
proof_techniques.description: The paper reviews a method in which two solutions are driven by the same noise and a C^2 Lyapunov function V of their difference is shown, via Itô's formula, to have negative drift. This yields decay of E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)], hence narrow convergence of transition laws from different initial conditions. If an invariant measure exists, this asymptotic coupling in law implies uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a uniqueness route that does not require strong Feller or irreducibility. It would require identifying a Lyapunov functional for the difference of two solutions to the singular-drift SPDE or to its approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Dissipativity and remote start
proof_techniques.description: The remote-start method constructs solutions on the whole real line and starts them at times s→-∞. Dissipativity gives exponential contraction between solutions started at different remote times, making X(0;s,y) Cauchy in L^1. The limiting random variable defines an invariant law, and the same contraction gives convergence toward it and uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful if the drift plus Dirichlet Laplacian can be placed into an m-dissipative framework or if the regularized drifts satisfy a uniform dissipativity estimate. It proves weak uniqueness/stability and does not rely on total-variation smoothing.

proof_techniques.name: Spectral verification for additive heat noise
proof_techniques.description: For diagonal parabolic operators, the paper reduces the key Gaussian range condition to an explicit sequence bound involving eigenvalues of A and Q. In the one-dimensional Dirichlet heat equation with Q=I, the summability and range conditions needed for strong Gaussian mixing are satisfied.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The linear part of the original SPDE is exactly the one-dimensional Dirichlet heat operator with space-time white noise. This supplies a ready-made nondegeneracy check for any argument comparing the nonlinear dynamics to the linear Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process.

other_useful_info: The paper defines strong asymptotic stability as convergence in total variation and weak asymptotic stability as narrow convergence against bounded continuous functions; both imply uniqueness of an invariant measure. It explicitly says that existence of invariant measures is not treated in the review. Section 2 notes that SPDEs with additive noise and drift terms having a potential may have explicitly describable invariant measures, citing Marcus [45-47], Kozlov [40], and Freidlin [25]; this pointer may be relevant for distributional drifts that are formal derivatives of bounded potentials. The paper also emphasizes that in infinite dimensions strong total-variation convergence can fail for degenerate noise even when a unique invariant measure exists and transition laws converge narrowly, so weak-stability methods can be essential. For multiplicative-noise equations, the paper cites an analogous dissipativity theorem in which the key condition involves Yosida approximations A_n=nA(nI-A)^{-1}: ⟨A_n(x-y)+f(x)-f(y),x-y⟩+||σ(x)-σ(y)||_{HS}^2 ≤ -ϖ||x-y||^2. Main related references identified by the review include Da Prato-Zabczyk [18,20] for stochastic equations and ergodicity in infinite dimensions, Chojnowska-Michalik-Goldys [8] and Gatarek-Goldys [28] for Kolmogorov/invariant-measure methods, Da Prato-Elworthy-Zabczyk [11] and Peszat-Zabczyk [60] for strong Feller derivative methods, Stettner [63] and Seidler [61] for the abstract equivalence theorem, and Mueller [59] for coupling methods for heat equations with noise.
why_relevant: This review collects uniqueness and stability criteria for invariant measures of nonlinear SPDEs, including strong Feller, irreducibility, recurrence, and convergence methods. It is useful for choosing the right abstract theorem for the present Markov semigroup.
